excerpt: The human composite consists of body, animal soul, and rational soul; the work of the believer's lifetime is to purify the lower parts through sharīʿa and to perfect the rational soul through gnosis, so that at death the noble form (al-ṣūra al-sharīfa) is ready to ascend while the residues are discarded.
source_location_hint: Doctrine of the tripartite human and the formation of the ṣūra sharīfa during life

excerpt: Yā Ḥāri Hamdān, man yamut yaranī / min muʾminin aw munāfiqin qubulā / yaʿrifunī wa-aʿrifuhu bi-ʿaynihi / wa-bi-ismihi wa-mā faʿalā — Whoever dies, O Ḥārith, shall see me at his end, believer or hypocrite alike; he shall know me and I shall know him by his name and his deeds.
source_location_hint: Famous verses addressed to al-Ḥārith b. ʿAbd Allāh al-Hamdānī, transmitted among the poetry of the Imam

excerpt: The human acquires successive souls in stages — the vegetative (nāmiya) from the lunar sphere, the sensitive (ḥissiya) at birth into the sensible world, and the rational (nāṭiqa) through reception of the covenant of allegiance — and only the rational soul can perfect the lower two and bind them into the noble form.
source_location_hint: Yanābīʿ on the hierarchy of souls and their progressive acquisition

excerpt: At death the noble form of the believer merges into the form of his upper limit (ḥadd ʿālī) and becomes subservient to its soul; the two then ascend, returning from limit to limit until they reach the temple (haykal) of the Imam, where the issue of the lāhūt is prepared.
source_location_hint: Doctrine of the merger of the ṣūra sharīfa with the ḥadd ʿālī and the upward return of souls

excerpt: The faculties of corporeal sense cannot endure even the light of the sun; how then should they bear the light of the spiritual realm, whose radiance is of a wholly different order?
source_authority: Chief dāʿī of the Fatimid daʿwa under al-Mustanṣir (d. 470/1078)
source_location_hint: Reflections on the disproportion between corporeal sense and spiritual light

excerpt: On the layered souls of the believer — vegetative (nāmiya), sensitive (ḥissiya), and airy (rīḥiya) — and the staged separation and ascent of the noble remainder after death.
source_authority: Second Ṭayyibī dāʿī muṭlaq (d. 557/1162); locus classicus of post-Fatimid Ṭayyibī cosmology and psychology of souls.
source_location_hint: Kanz al-Walad, sections on the nufūs and their ascent

excerpt: On the role of the celestial spheres in purifying the souls of the believers and the constitution of the Imam's nāsūt as the receptacle in which the lāhūt is received.
source_authority: Foremost philosophical theologian of the Fatimid daʿwa (d. ca. 411/1020); his Rāḥat al-ʿAql is the systematic treatise on Ismaili cosmology and imamology.
source_location_hint: Rāḥat al-ʿAql, mashrū on the Imam and the relation of nāsūt to lāhūt

excerpt: On the purity of the ahl al-bayt and the āyat al-taṭhīr (Q 33:33) as the Qurʾanic ground for their divinely willed purification from defilement.
source_authority: Chief jurist of the Fatimid caliphate under al-Muʿizz (d. 363/974); Daʿāʾim al-Islām is the foundational legal-doctrinal corpus of the Fatimid daʿwa.
source_location_hint: Daʿāʾim al-Islām, Kitāb al-Walāya, chapters on the merits and purity of the Prophet's family

excerpt: Doctrine that the believers' purified souls (rīḥiyāt) are gathered into and constitute the luminous haykal of the Imam, the rīḥiyāt and ṣuwarāt severed at death being reunited at the imamic locus.
source_authority: second Ṭayyibī dāʿī muṭlaq (d. 557/1162); canonical exposition of the doctrine of the haykal nūrānī
source_location_hint: sections on the constitution of the imamic haykal from the souls of the muʾminūn

excerpt: Hierarchy of the Intellects emanated from the First Originated, descending through the ten ḥudūd, with the Tenth Intellect (al-ʿaql al-ʿāshir) standing in relation to the human/terrestrial world and its believers.
source_authority: Fatimid ḥujja and chief doctrinal authority under al-Ḥākim; canonical exposition of the ten-intellect cosmology
source_location_hint: the mashrā (chapters) on the emanation of the Intellects and the rank of the Tenth Intellect over the world of nature

excerpt: The soul's gradual purification across the planetary spheres, culminating in the differentiation of the sensitive and vegetative souls under solar and lunar influence, reflects the Neoplatonic ascent doctrine developed by al-Sijistānī, in which the human soul attains its rational and vital faculties by stages through the celestial mediations.
source_location_hint: On the soul's ascent through the celestial spheres and its acquisition of faculties

excerpt: The doctrine that divine providence (ʿināya ilāhiyya) wrests a soul out of refined corporeal residue, working through the cosmic hierarchy and the mediating ranks (ḥudūd), is articulated by al-Kirmani in his exposition of how the lower limit (ḥadd dānī) ascends toward the upper limit (ḥadd ʿālī) by the influence of the higher intellects.
source_location_hint: On the hierarchy of intellects and the mediation of divine providence

excerpt: The esoteric reading of Qurʾanic resurrection imagery — that the revival of decayed bones signifies the reassembly of subtle bodies and ranks within the daʿwa across cosmic cycles — exemplifies the taʾwīl method by which Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān refers outward Qurʾanic figures to inward hierarchical realities.
source_location_hint: On the principle of taʾwīl applied to resurrection and bodily revival

excerpt: The Imam's body as the locus in which the subtle remnants (raīḥiyyāt) of the believers are gathered, such that the visible form of the Imam mediates between nāsūt (corporeality) and lāhūt (divinity), corresponds to al-Kirmani's account of the Imam as the supreme ḥadd through whom the lower ranks rise toward their perfection.
source_location_hint: On the rank of the Imam and the mediation between nāsūt and lāhūt

excerpt: The rational soul, once it has been actualised by accepting the daʿwa, cannot revert to the privation that preceded it; what it has won of intellectual perfection persists through the cycles and is the seed (khamīra) by which it ascends.
source_location_hint: Sixth Sūr — on the soul's perfection and its persistence beyond bodily separation

excerpt: The bāb and the dāʿī are conjoined to the ḥadd of the Imam, so that whoever attains union with the Imam in this cycle is brought directly to the ḥaḍra without traversing the intermediate ḥudūd one by one.
source_location_hint: Majlis on the rank of the martyr and the direct ascent to the Imamic presence

excerpt: The ranks of the daʿwa are ordered as the gradations of being itself: mustajīb, muʾmin, mukāsir, maʾdhūn, dāʿī, ḥujja, bāb — each station mirroring a ḥadd of the spiritual world above and a ḥadd of the corporeal world below.
source_location_hint: Discussion of the seven ḥudūd of the daʿwa and their correspondence to the intelligible ḥudūd

excerpt: The bāb stands in the cycle as the First Intellect stands in the spiritual world: the immediate organ by which the Originator's command is received and dispensed to those below.
source_location_hint: On the correspondence between the rank of the bāb and the rank of the al-Munbaʿith al-Awwal

excerpt: The intellects are ten, and from the perfection of the number ten proceed all the gradations of the religious world; whoever traverses the ten degrees of a station has completed its kamāl thānī and is fit to receive the next.
source_authority: Iranian dāʿī of the early Fatimid daʿwa (d. after 361/971)
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the ten intellects and the perfection of the decad

excerpt: Kirmānī sets out the layered constitution of the Imam — uniting noble form, soul, lāhūt, and the two gradations of nāsūt — as the cosmological key to why the corporeal residue alone remains in the grave while the higher constituents ascend.
source_authority: Fatimid dāʿī al-duʿāt; foundational systematic work of Fatimid cosmology
source_location_hint: Mashraʿ on the ḥudūd of the dīn and the constitution of the imamate

excerpt: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān's esoteric exegesis treats the prophets and Imams as a single continuous light, each Imam absorbing into himself the spiritual reality of his predecessors, so that the imamic line forms one indivisible nūr.
source_authority: Chief jurist of the Fatimid caliph al-Muʿizz; foundational Fatimid taʾwīl authority
source_location_hint: Sections on the continuity of nubuwwa and imāma

excerpt: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān's juristic compendium codifies the ziyāra of the Prophet and the Imams, teaching that the visitor draws benefit from the higher rank of the one visited and that the prayers offered at the grave are heard and answered.
source_authority: Chief jurist of the Fatimid state; canonical Fatimid law
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Janāʾiz / Bāb ziyārat al-qubūr
